k : Type u_1

M_ : ι → Submodule k M
inst✝ : IsFilterComplete M_
α : Type u_4
⊢ IsFilterComplete fun i => Submodule.pi Set.univ fun x => M_ i

isFilterComplete_of_le fun i j => by
    have h₁ : ∃ (k : ι), M_ k ≥ M_ i ∧ M_ k ≥ M_ j := by sorry
    obtain ⟨k, hk_i, hk_j⟩ := h₁
    have h₂ : (fun i : ι => Submodule.pi Set.univ (fun _ : α => M_ i)) i ≤ (fun i : ι => Submodule.pi Set.univ (fun _ : α => M_ i)) k := by sorry
    have h₃ : (fun i : ι => Submodule.pi Set.univ (fun _ : α => M_ i)) j ≤ (fun i : ι => Submodule.pi Set.univ (fun _ : α => M_ i)) k := by sorry
    exact ⟨k, h₂, h₃⟩

{
    refine' ⟨fun i j h => _, fun i => _, fun i => _⟩
    · -- The inclusion part of the filtered inductive
      -- If i ≤ j, then we need to show that the submodules are embedded corectly
      -- Since `Submodule.pi Set.univ` is over the same set, it's easy to include it.
      exact Submodule.pi_mono (fun x => by simp_all [Set.mem_univ])
    · -- The identity part of the filtered inductive
      -- This is trivially true by the reflexivity of the inclusion relation.
      by simp
    · -- The part where we need to find a common upper bound for any two indices.
      -- Because ι is filter complete and `Submodule.pi Set.univ` is unique for each i and j,
      -- we can find a common upper bound.
      refine' ⟨_, _⟩
      · -- Proof that i ≤ _, which is trivial.
        by exact le_rfl
      · -- Proof that the common upper bound is unique.
        -- It is trivially true because the condition is for any two indices, we can take max(i,j) or i+j etc.
        -- In this case, the common upper bound is their max, but we do not need this.
        -- We can directly use the filter completeness of ι.
        by simp_all [IsFilterComplete]
  }

{
    complete : ∀ (ι₀ : Set ι), BddAbove ι₀ → ∃ (c : ι), ∀ (i : ι), i ∈ ι₀ → (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) i ≤ (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) c :=
      by
      intro ι₀ hι
      obtain ⟨c, hc⟩ := M_.complete ι₀ hι
      refine' ⟨c, _⟩
      intro i hi
      have h₁ : M_ i ≤ M_ c := by
        exact hc i hi
      have h₂ : Submodule.pi Set.univ (fun _ : α => M_ i) ≤ Submodule.pi Set.univ (fun _ : α => M_ c) := by
        apply Submodule.pi_mono
        intro x _
        exact h₁
      simpa using h₂
  }

-- Since the product of submodules is a submodule and Lean's typeclass system allows us to deduce that the product of filtered modules is filtered,
  -- we can directly use the `infer_instance` tactic to infer the `IsFilterComplete` instance for the product.
  infer_instance

    infer_instance

{
    stable := by
      -- Show that the union of (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) is the entire module.
      have h₁ : (⨆ i, (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) i) = ⊤ := by
        -- Use the fact that M_ is filter complete and the pi construction preserves the union.
        have h₂ : (⨆ i, (M_ i : Submodule k M)) = ⊤ := by
          exact IsFilterComplete.stable
        calc
          (⨆ i, (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) i) =
              ⨆ i, Submodule.pi Set.univ (fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) := by simp [Function.funext_iff]
          _ = Submodule.pi Set.univ (fun _ : α ↦ ⨆ i, (M_ i : Submodule k M)) := by
            -- Use the property of pi and supremum.
            rw [iSup_pi]
          _ = Submodule.pi Set.univ (fun _ : α ↦ ⊤) := by
            -- Use the fact that the supremum of M_ is the entire module.
            congr
            <;> ext
            <;> simp_all [iSup_const]
          _ = ⊤ := by
            -- Show that the pi of top modules is the top module.
            ext x
            simp [Submodule.pi.mem_def]
            <;> aesop
      -- Use the fact that the supremum is the entire module to conclude stability.
      exact h₁
    directed := by
      -- Show that the collection is directed.
      intro i j
      have h₁ : i ≤ j → (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) i ≤ (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) j := by
        intro hij
        have h₂ : M_ i ≤ M_ j := IsFilterComplete.le hij
        have h₃ : (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) i ≤ (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) j := by
          -- Use the fact that M_i ≤ M_j to show the pi construction preserves this.
          ext x
          simp only [Pi.mem_univ, Set.mem_setOf_eq, fun_i] at *
          exact Submodule.val_mono h₂
        exact h₃
      by_cases h : i ≤ j
      · exact h₁ h
      · -- If i is not less than or equal to j, we can use the fact that the collection is directed.
        have h₂ : j ≤ i := by
          -- Since the collection is directed, we can find a common upper bound.
          have h₃ : i ≤ i := le_refl i
          have h₄ : j ≤ j := le_refl j
          -- Use the fact that the collection is directed to find a common upper bound.
          -- This is a contradiction because we assumed i is not less than or equal to j.
          exfalso
          -- Since the collection is directed, we can find a common upper bound.
          -- However, in this case, we need to use the fact that the collection is not directed.
          have h₅ : IsFilterComplete M_ := ‹_›
          have h₆ : i ≤ i := le_refl i
          have h₇ : j ≤ j := le_refl j
          -- Use the fact that the collection is directed to find a common upper bound.
          -- However, in this case, we need to use the fact that the collection is not directed.
          simp_all [IsFilterComplete]
          <;>
          (try contradiction) <;>
          (try aesop) <;>
          (try omega)
        exact h₁ h₂
    separate := by
      -- Show that the collection is separating.
      intro f g hf hg hfg
      have h₁ : ∃ i, f i ∉ (M_ i : Set M) ∨ g i ∉ (M_ i : Set M) := by
        by_contra! h₂
        have h₃ : f = g := by
          funext i
          have h₄ : f i ∈ (M_ i : Set M) := by
            have h₅ : f i ∈ (M_ i : Set M) := by
              have h₆ : f i ∈ (M_ i : Set M) := by
                have h₇ : f i ∈ (M_ i : Set M) := by
                  simpa using h₂ i |>.1
                exact h₇
              exact h₆
            exact h₅
          have h₅ : g i ∈ (M_ i : Set M) := by
            have h₆ : g i ∈ (M_ i : Set M) := by
              have h₇ : g i ∈ (M_ i : Set M) := by
                simpa using h₂ i |>.2
              exact h₇
            exact h₆
          have h₆ : f i = g i := by
            have h₇ : (f i : M) = (g i : M) := by
              have h₈ : f i = g i := by
                simp_all [Submodule.mem_carrier]
                <;>
                aesop
              exact h₈
            exact h₇
          exact h₆
        simp_all
      obtain ⟨i, hi⟩ := h₁
      refine' ⟨i, _⟩
      have h₂ : f i ∉ (M_ i : Set M) ∨ g i ∉ (M_ i : Set M) := hi
      cases h₂ with
      | inl h₂ =>
        -- If f i is not in M_i, then we are done.
        have h₃ : f i ∉ (M_ i : Set M) := h₂
        have h₄ : f ∉ (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) i := by
          intro h₅
          have h₆ : f ∈ (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) i := h₅
          have h₇ : f i ∈ (M_ i : Set M) := by
            have h₈ : f ∈ (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) i := h₆
            have h₉ : f i ∈ (M_ i : Set M) := by
              simp only [Set.mem_setOf_eq, Submodule.mem_pi] at h₈ ⊢
              have h₁₀ : ∀ (x : α), f x ∈ (M_ i : Set M) := by
                intro x
                specialize h₈ x
                simp_all [Set.mem_univ]
                <;> aesop
              specialize h₁₀ i
              simp_all [Set.mem_univ]
              <;> aesop
            exact h₉
          exact h₃ h₇
        exact h₄
      | inr h₂ =>
        -- If g i is not in M_i, then we are done.
        have h₃ : g i ∉ (M_ i : Set M) := h₂
        have h₄ : g ∉ (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) i := by
          intro h₅
          have h₆ : g ∈ (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) i := h₅
          have h₇ : g i ∈ (M_ i : Set M) := by
            have h₈ : g ∈ (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) i := h₆
            have h₉ : g i ∈ (M_ i : Set M) := by
              simp only [Set.mem_setOf_eq, Submodule.mem_pi] at h₈ ⊢
              have h₁₀ : ∀ (x : α), g x ∈ (M_ i : Set M) := by
                intro x
                specialize h₈ x
                simp_all [Set.mem_univ]
                <;> aesop
              specialize h₁₀ i
              simp_all [Set.mem_univ]
              <;> aesop
            exact h₉
          exact h₃ h₇
        exact h₄
  }

have h_main : IsFilterComplete (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi (Set.univ : Set α) (fun _ : α ↦ M_ i)) := by
    classical
    -- Use the fact that M_ is filter complete to find a suitable index i in any filtered set s
    rw [IsFilterComplete]
    intro s hs
    -- Obtain the index i and the property from the filter completeness of M_
    have h₁ : ∃ (i : ι), i ∈ s ∧ ∀ (j : ι), j ∈ s → i ≤ j → M_ i = M_ j := by
      have h₂ : ∃ (i : ι), i ∈ s ∧ ∀ (j : ι), j ∈ s → i ≤ j → M_ i = M_ j := by
        -- Use the filter completeness of M_ to get the required index i
        have h₃ : IsFilterComplete M_ := inferInstance
        have h₄ : ∃ (i : ι), i ∈ s ∧ ∀ (j : ι), j ∈ s → i ≤ j → M_ i = M_ j := by
          specialize h₃ s hs
          obtain ⟨i, hi, h₅⟩ := h₃
          refine' ⟨i, hi, _⟩
          intro j hj hij
          exact h₅ j hj hij
        exact h₄
      exact h₂
    -- Obtain the index i and its properties
    obtain ⟨i, hi, h₂⟩ := h₁
    refine' ⟨i, hi, _⟩
    -- Show that for all j ≥ i in s, the pi submodules are equal
    intro j hj hij
    have h₃ : M_ i = M_ j := h₂ j hj hij
    -- Use the fact that M_ i = M_ j to show that the pi submodules are equal
    have h₄ : Submodule.pi (Set.univ : Set α) (fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) = Submodule.pi (Set.univ : Set α) (fun _ : α ↦ M_ j) := by
      apply Submodule.ext
      intro x
      simp only [Submodule.mem_pi]
      constructor <;> intro h <;>
      (try simp_all) <;>
      (try {
        have h₅ : ∀ (a : α), x a ∈ M_ i := by simpa using h
        have h₆ : ∀ (a : α), x a ∈ M_ j := by
          intro a
          have h₇ : x a ∈ M_ i := h₅ a
          have h₈ : M_ i = M_ j := h₃
          rw [h₈] at h₇
          exact h₇
        exact h₆
      }) <;>
      (try {
        have h₅ : ∀ (a : α), x a ∈ M_ j := by simpa using h
        have h₆ : ∀ (a : α), x a ∈ M_ i := by
          intro a
          have h₇ : x a ∈ M_ j := h₅ a
          have h₈ : M_ i = M_ j := h₃
          rw [h₈] at *
          exact h₇
        exact h₆
      })
    -- Conclude that the pi submodules are equal
    rw [h₄]
  -- Use the main result to conclude the proof
  simpa using h_main

by
    have I : IsFilterComplete (fun (i : ι) => Submodule.pi Set.univ (fun _ : α => M_ i)) :=
      by
        -- We need to show that the product of the filtered submodules is filtered.
        -- This involves showing that the product of the submodules is the submodule of the product.
        -- We use the fact that the product of submodules is a submodule and the filter completeness of M_.
        -- The key step is to realize that the product of the filtered submodules is filtered because each component is filtered.
        -- We use the definition of IsFilterComplete and the properties of products of submodules.
        -- Specifically, we use the fact that the product of submodules is a submodule and the filter completeness of M_.
        -- The proof involves showing that the product of the submodules is the submodule of the product.
        -- This is done by showing that the product of the submodules is the submodule of the product.
        refine' ⟨fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ ↦ M_ i, ?_⟩
        -- We need to show that the product of the submodules is the submodule of the product.
        -- This is done by showing that the product of the submodules is the submodule of the product.
        -- We use the fact that the product of submodules is a submodule and the filter completeness of M_.
        -- The key step is to realize that the product of the filtered submodules is filtered because each component is filtered.
        -- We use the definition of IsFilterComplete and the properties of products of submodules.
        -- Specifically, we use the fact that the product of submodules is a submodule and the filter completeness of M_.
        -- The proof involves showing that the product of the submodules is the submodule of the product.
        -- This is done by showing that the product of the submodules is the submodule of the product.
        simp [IsFilterComplete]
        <;>
        exact inferInstance
      exact I
